title: Check if Linear Transformation is Singular
description: Algorithm to determine if a linear transformation represented by square matrix is singular
code:
// Input: n×n matrix A
// Output: Boolean - true if singular, false if non-singular

FUNCTION isSingular(A, n):
 det ← calculateDeterminant(A, n)
 IF det == 0 THEN
 RETURN true // Singular
 ELSE
 RETURN false // Non-singular (invertible)

FUNCTION calculateDeterminant(A, n):
 IF n == 1 THEN
 RETURN A[0][0]
 IF n == 2 THEN
 RETURN A[0][0]*A[1][1] - A[0][1]*A[1][0]
 
 det ← 0
 FOR j ← 0 TO n-1:
 sign ← (-1)^j
 subMatrix ← getMinor(A, 0, j, n)
 det ← det + sign * A[0][j] * calculateDeterminant(subMatrix, n-1)
 RETURN det
explanation: Uses cofactor expansion to compute determinant. If det=0, matrix is singular. For large matrices, Gaussian elimination is more efficient.

title: Find Kernel (Null Space) Basis
description: Algorithm to find basis of kernel using Gaussian elimination
explanation: Uses RREF to solve Ax=0. Free variables give parameters for kernel vectors. Each free variable creates one basis vector.

title: Verify One-to-One Property
description: Algorithm to check if transformation is one-to-one using kernel
explanation: One-to-one (injective) means kernel contains only zero vector. By Rank-Nullity, nullity = n - rank. If nullity = 0, transformation is one-to-one.

title: Inverse of Linear Transformation
description: Algorithm to find inverse of non-singular transformation using Gauss-Jordan
explanation: Gauss-Jordan elimination transforms [A|I] to [I|A⁻¹]. If pivot becomes zero mid-process, matrix is singular (no inverse).
